terms.definition: The angle measure of a straight line or half turn; the sum of angles in any triangle.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: Angles on a straight line add up to 180 degrees.
terms.common_errors: Confusing 180° (straight line) with 360° (full turn).

terms.definition: Standard form notation where a number between 1 and 10 is multiplied by a power of 10 to represent very large or small numbers.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: Write 45,000 in standard form: 4.5 × 10⁴.
terms.common_errors: Making 'a' less than 1 or greater than 10.

terms.definition: The distance of a number from zero on a number line, always positive; written as |x|.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: The absolute value of -7 is 7.
terms.common_errors: Thinking absolute value changes the sign of positive numbers.

terms.definition: How close a measured or calculated value is to the true value.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: Give your answer to an appropriate degree of accuracy.

terms.definition: The real-life measurement of an object, as opposed to a scaled representation.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: The actual size of the room is 5m × 4m; the scale drawing shows it as 5cm × 4cm.

terms.definition: The number that, when added to a given number, gives zero; for any number n, its additive inverse is -n.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: The additive inverse of 5 is -5 because 5 + (-5) = 0.

terms.definition: Thinking about mathematical relationships in terms of addition and subtraction (difference), as opposed to multiplicative reasoning.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: Finding the difference between two quantities uses additive reasoning.
terms.common_errors: Using additive reasoning when multiplicative reasoning is needed.

terms.definition: Next to each other; sharing a common side or vertex.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: In trigonometry, the adjacent side is next to the angle (and not the hypotenuse).
terms.common_errors: Confusing adjacent with opposite in trigonometry.

terms.definition: Involving or relating to algebra; using letters and symbols to represent quantities.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: Write an algebraic expression for 'three more than n': n + 3.

terms.definition: A formal demonstration that a mathematical statement is always true, using algebraic manipulation.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: Prove algebraically that the sum of two consecutive even numbers is always even.
terms.common_errors: Using specific numbers instead of general algebraic expressions.

terms.definition: The process of rewriting an algebraic expression in a simpler equivalent form by collecting like terms and applying laws.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: Simplify 3x + 2y + 5x - y = 8x + y.
terms.common_errors: Combining unlike terms (e.g. adding 3x and 2y).

terms.definition: A pair of angles on the same side of a transversal between parallel lines; they add up to 180°. Also called co-interior angles.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: Allied angles sum to 180°.
terms.common_errors: Confusing allied angles with alternate angles.

terms.definition: To assign or distribute values, resources, or quantities according to a rule.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: Allocate the budget equally across the three projects.

terms.definition: A pair of equal angles on opposite sides of a transversal between parallel lines; they form a Z-shape.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: Alternate angles are equal when lines are parallel.
terms.common_errors: Applying alternate angle rules when lines are not parallel.

terms.definition: A line that divides an angle into two equal parts.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: Construct the angle bisector of this 60° angle using a compass.
terms.common_errors: Not keeping the compass width the same for both arcs.

terms.definition: The number of degrees a shape turns during a rotation.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: The shape has been rotated 90° anticlockwise — the angle of rotation is 90°.
terms.common_errors: Not specifying the direction (clockwise/anticlockwise).

terms.definition: The total of all interior angles in a polygon; for a triangle it is 180°, for a quadrilateral 360°.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: The angle sum of a pentagon is (5-2) × 180° = 540°.
terms.common_errors: Using the wrong formula — it's (n-2) × 180° for n sides.

terms.definition: A calculator button that recalls the result of the previous calculation.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: Press ANS to use your last answer in the next calculation.

terms.definition: Turning in the opposite direction to clock hands; from right to left.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: Rotate the shape 90° anti-clockwise about the origin.
terms.common_errors: Confusing clockwise with anti-clockwise.

terms.definition: To use a mathematical rule, formula, or technique to solve a problem.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: Apply Pythagoras' theorem to find the missing side.

terms.definition: An increase in value over time, often expressed as a percentage.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: A house bought for £200,000 with 5% annual appreciation is worth £210,000 after one year.
terms.common_errors: Confusing appreciation (increase) with depreciation (decrease).

terms.definition: A solution that is close to but not exactly the true answer, often found by trial and improvement or iteration.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: By trial and improvement, x ≈ 2.3 is an approximate solution to x³ = 12.

terms.definition: A value that is close to the exact answer, used when precision is not required or not possible.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: π ≈ 3.14 is an approximation.

terms.definition: The space enclosed by a circle, calculated using the formula A = πr².
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: The area of a circle with radius 5 cm is π × 5² = 25π ≈ 78.5 cm².
terms.common_errors: Using diameter instead of radius in the formula.

terms.definition: A sequence where each term is found by adding a constant value (common difference) to the previous term.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: 5, 8, 11, 14 is an arithmetic sequence with common difference 3.
terms.common_errors: Confusing with geometric sequences (constant ratio).

terms.term: as one increases the other decreases
terms.definition: A description of inverse proportion or negative correlation between two variables.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: Speed and journey time: as one increases the other decreases.

terms.definition: Angle-Side-Angle: a congruence criterion where two angles and the included side are equal.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: These triangles are congruent by ASA — two angles and the side between them match.
terms.common_errors: Confusing ASA with AAS.

terms.definition: Something taken to be true without proof, stated at the start of a problem or model.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: Our assumption is that the dice is fair.

terms.definition: A line that a curve approaches but never reaches, getting infinitely close without touching.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: The graph of y = 1/x has asymptotes at x = 0 and y = 0.
terms.common_errors: Thinking the curve eventually reaches the asymptote.

terms.definition: The ability to recall mathematical facts and perform procedures effortlessly and without conscious thought.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: Automaticity with times tables frees up working memory for harder problems.

terms.definition: Pythagoras' theorem: in a right-angled triangle, the square of the hypotenuse equals the sum of the squares of the other two sides.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: Use a² + b² = c² to find the hypotenuse: if a=3 and b=4, then c=5.
terms.common_errors: Forgetting that c must be the hypotenuse (longest side).

terms.definition: A visual representation using rectangular bars to model mathematical relationships, especially part-whole and comparison problems.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: Draw a bar model to represent: the total is 45, one part is 28, find the other part.

terms.definition: Our number system that uses ten digits (0-9) and place values based on powers of 10.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: In base ten, the digit 3 in 300 represents 3 × 10².

terms.definition: A reasoning word used to justify mathematical statements and connect conclusions to evidence.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: The answer is 7 because 3 + 4 = 7.
terms.common_errors: Not providing mathematical justification after 'because'.

terms.definition: The option that gives the most value for money, determined by comparing unit prices.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: Which is the best buy: 3 for £1.20 or 5 for £1.75?
terms.common_errors: Not converting to the same unit before comparing.

terms.definition: Not fair or representative; in probability, a biased event has unequal probabilities for different outcomes.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: This spinner is biased — it lands on red more often than blue.
terms.common_errors: Assuming all experiments are fair without checking.

terms.definition: An algebraic expression with exactly two terms, such as (x + 3) or (2a - 5).
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: Expand the product of two binomials: (x + 2)(x + 5).
terms.common_errors: Forgetting to multiply all terms — must get four terms before simplifying.

terms.definition: To divide exactly into two equal parts.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: Bisect the line segment AB to find its midpoint.
terms.common_errors: Not being accurate enough when constructing a bisector.

terms.definition: Data that involves two variables measured on the same set of items, often displayed on a scatter graph.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: Height and arm span is bivariate data — plot it on a scatter graph.

terms.definition: A phrase emphasising that the same operation must be applied to both sides of an equation to maintain equality.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: Subtract 5 from both sides: x + 5 = 12 becomes x = 7.
terms.common_errors: Only applying an operation to one side of the equation.

terms.definition: The upper and lower limits of a rounded value; the range within which the true value lies.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: If a length is 4.5 cm to 1 d.p., the bounds are 4.45 ≤ x < 4.55.
terms.common_errors: Using the wrong inequality for the upper bound.

terms.definition: Symbols ( ) used to group terms in expressions, indicating these should be calculated first.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: Expand the bracket: 3(x + 4) = 3x + 12.
terms.common_errors: Only multiplying the first term inside the bracket.

terms.definition: To decompose a problem or calculation into smaller, more manageable steps.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: Break down the problem: first find the area, then convert the units.

terms.definition: A plan for how money will be spent over a period of time; used in financial mathematics contexts.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: Calculate whether this budget covers all the expenses.

terms.definition: An informal name for co-interior (allied) angles formed by a transversal crossing parallel lines, making a C-shape.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: C-angles add up to 180°.
terms.common_errors: Confusing C-angles (co-interior, add to 180°) with Z-angles (alternate, equal).

terms.definition: An electronic device for performing arithmetic and more complex mathematical operations.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: Use a calculator to find √7 correct to 2 decimal places.
terms.common_errors: Not using brackets on a calculator when needed.

terms.definition: An uppercase letter used in algebra to represent specific points, shapes, or sets.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: Label the vertices of the triangle A, B, and C.

terms.definition: A flat surface defined by two perpendicular number lines (x-axis and y-axis) meeting at the origin.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: Plot the points on the Cartesian plane.

terms.definition: Data that can be sorted into named groups or categories rather than measured numerically.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: Favourite colour is categorical data; it cannot be averaged.
terms.common_errors: Trying to calculate a mean for categorical data.

terms.definition: A statistical measure representing the centre or typical value of a data set (mean, median, or mode).
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: Compare the central tendency of two data sets using their medians.
terms.common_errors: Always using the mean — the median is better for skewed data.

terms.definition: The fixed point from which a shape is enlarged; all points move away from (or towards) this point by the scale factor.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: Enlarge the triangle by scale factor 2 from centre of enlargement (0, 0).
terms.common_errors: Measuring distances from the wrong point.

terms.definition: The fixed point around which a shape rotates during a rotation transformation.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: Rotate the shape 90° clockwise about the centre of rotation (2, 3).
terms.common_errors: Rotating about the wrong point.

terms.definition: To rearrange a formula so that a different variable is isolated on one side.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: Change the subject of v = u + at to make t the subject: t = (v - u)/a.
terms.common_errors: Applying inverse operations in the wrong order.

terms.definition: A range of values used to group continuous data in a frequency table or histogram.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: The class intervals are 0-10, 10-20, 20-30.
terms.common_errors: Creating overlapping intervals (e.g. 0-10, 10-20 — where does 10 go?).

terms.definition: The range covered by a single class interval, found by subtracting the lower boundary from the upper boundary.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: The class width of 10-20 is 10.
terms.common_errors: Using unequal class widths without adjusting the frequency density.

terms.definition: Angles on the same side of a transversal between parallel lines; they sum to 180°.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: Co-interior angles add up to 180° when lines are parallel.
terms.common_errors: Thinking they are equal instead of supplementary.

terms.definition: To gather data or to combine like algebraic terms.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: Collect like terms: 3x + 5 + 2x - 1 = 5x + 4.

terms.definition: To simplify an algebraic expression by adding or subtracting terms with the same variable and power.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: Collect like terms: 4a + 3b + 2a - b = 6a + 2b.
terms.common_errors: Combining terms with different variables or powers.

terms.definition: The symbol : used to write ratios, separating the quantities being compared.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: The ratio 3:5 uses a colon to separate the two values.

terms.definition: Two or more events happening together; their probabilities are found using tree diagrams or two-way tables.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: Find the probability of both combined events: rolling a 6 and flipping heads.
terms.common_errors: Adding probabilities instead of multiplying for independent combined events.

terms.definition: Shared by two or more numbers; used in common factor, common multiple, common denominator.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: Find the common factors of 12 and 18.

terms.definition: The constant multiplier between consecutive terms in a geometric sequence.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: In the sequence 2, 6, 18, 54, the common ratio is 3.
terms.common_errors: Confusing common ratio with common difference.

terms.definition: The set of all elements not in a given set; in probability, P(not A) = 1 - P(A).
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: If P(rain) = 0.3, the complement P(no rain) = 0.7.
terms.common_errors: Confusing complement (probability) with complementary angles (sum to 90°).

terms.definition: Two angles that add together to make exactly 90°.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: 30° and 60° are complementary angles.
terms.common_errors: Confusing complementary (90°) with supplementary (180°).

terms.definition: A shape made by combining two or more simpler shapes, whose area or perimeter requires breaking it apart.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: Find the area of this composite shape by splitting it into a rectangle and triangle.
terms.common_errors: Missing a section when splitting up the shape.

terms.definition: A shape formed from two or more basic shapes joined together.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: Split the compound shape into rectangles to find the total area.
terms.common_errors: Double-counting overlapping regions.

terms.definition: A unit of measurement derived from two or more basic units, such as km/h (speed) or g/cm³ (density).
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: Speed is a compound unit: distance divided by time.
terms.common_errors: Not keeping units consistent when calculating.

terms.definition: A final statement or answer reached through logical reasoning or calculation.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: In conclusion, the triangle must be right-angled because 3² + 4² = 5².

terms.definition: The degree of certainty in a result, often related to sample size and data quality.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: A larger sample gives more confidence in the result.

terms.definition: The property of having the same shape and size; two congruent shapes are identical.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: Prove congruence using one of the criteria: SSS, SAS, ASA, or RHS.
terms.common_errors: Confusing congruence (same size) with similarity (same shape, different size).

terms.definition: Two triangles that are exactly the same shape and size, proved using SSS, SAS, ASA, or RHS.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: These triangles are congruent by SAS — two sides and the included angle match.

terms.definition: Producing the same or similar results repeatedly; low spread in data.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: Player A has more consistency — their scores have a smaller range.

terms.definition: Showing little variation; reliable and predictable results.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: The data is consistent — most values are close to the mean.

terms.definition: The fixed ratio k in a proportional relationship; y = kx (direct) or y = k/x (inverse).
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: If y = 3x, the constant of proportionality is 3.
terms.common_errors: Forgetting to find k before using the formula.

terms.definition: In inverse proportion, the value of xy that stays the same as x and y change.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: If xy = 24, when x doubles, y halves — the constant product is 24.

terms.definition: A term in an expression that has no variable; a fixed number.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: In 3x² + 5x + 7, the constant term is 7.

terms.definition: Drawing shapes and lines accurately using only a ruler and pair of compasses.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: Construct the perpendicular bisector of line AB.
terms.common_errors: Using a protractor when only ruler and compasses are allowed.

terms.definition: Data that can take any value within a range, not just whole numbers; measured rather than counted.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: Height is continuous data — you could be 152.3 cm tall.
terms.common_errors: Confusing continuous (measured) with discrete (counted).

terms.definition: Two numbers (x, y) that together describe a specific point on a grid.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: The coordinate pair (4, -2) means go 4 right and 2 down.
terms.common_errors: Reversing the x and y values.

terms.definition: A statistical relationship between two variables shown on a scatter graph; can be positive, negative, or none.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: There is a positive correlation between hours of study and test scores.
terms.common_errors: Assuming correlation means causation.

terms.definition: In the same relative position; matching parts of congruent or similar shapes.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: In similar triangles, corresponding sides are in the same ratio.
terms.common_errors: Matching the wrong sides or angles.

terms.definition: Angles in the same position at each intersection where a transversal crosses parallel lines; they are equal. F-shape pattern.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: Corresponding angles are equal when lines are parallel.
terms.common_errors: Confusing corresponding angles with alternate angles.

terms.definition: Sides in similar or congruent shapes that are in the same relative position.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: The ratio of corresponding sides in these similar triangles is 2:3.
terms.common_errors: Not identifying which sides correspond.

terms.definition: A trigonometric ratio: in a right-angled triangle, cosine of an angle = adjacent side ÷ hypotenuse.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.common_errors: Confusing cosine with sine — remember CAH (Cos = Adjacent/Hypotenuse).

terms.definition: The inverse cosine function; finds the angle when given the cosine ratio.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: If cos(x) = 0.5, then x = cos⁻¹(0.5) = 60°.
terms.common_errors: Confusing cos⁻¹(x) with 1/cos(x).

terms.definition: Data or objects that can be counted in whole numbers; discrete.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: The number of students is countable — you can't have 3.5 students.

terms.definition: A rule or test that must be satisfied; plural: criteria.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: The SAS criterion proves these triangles are congruent.
terms.common_errors: Using 'criteria' as singular — it's plural.

terms.definition: The 2-D shape revealed when a 3-D object is cut through by a flat plane.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: The cross-section of a cylinder is a circle.
terms.common_errors: Confusing cross-section with surface area.

terms.definition: The area of the 2-D shape formed when cutting through a 3-D object perpendicular to its length.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: Volume of a prism = cross-sectional area × length.

terms.definition: The value that, when multiplied by itself three times, gives the original number; written as ∛.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: The cube root of 27 is 3, because 3 × 3 × 3 = 27.
terms.common_errors: Confusing cube root with square root.

terms.definition: A unit of volume equal to a 1cm × 1cm × 1cm cube, written as cm³.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: The volume of this cuboid is 120 cubic centimetres.
terms.common_errors: Confusing cm³ with cm².

terms.definition: A unit of volume equal to a 1m × 1m × 1m cube, written as m³.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: The room has a volume of 48 cubic metres.

terms.definition: A smooth, continuously bending line; the graph of a non-linear function.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: The graph of y = x² is a curve called a parabola.
terms.common_errors: Connecting points with straight lines when a smooth curve is needed.

terms.definition: A polygon with 10 sides and 10 angles.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: A regular decagon has 10 equal sides.
terms.common_errors: Confusing decagon (10 sides) with dodecagon (12 sides).

terms.definition: A decrease in value over time, often by a constant percentage; exponential decay.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: Radioactive decay: the amount halves every 30 years.
terms.common_errors: Confusing decay (decrease) with growth (increase).

terms.definition: A decimal value that is close to but not exactly equal to the true value, often used for irrational numbers.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: 3.14159 is a decimal approximation of π.

terms.definition: A fraction whose denominator is a power of 10, or equivalently, a number written in decimal notation.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: 0.75 is a decimal fraction equivalent to 75/100.

terms.definition: A decimal used to calculate percentage increase or decrease in one step (e.g. 1.05 for 5% increase).
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: To increase by 15%, multiply by the decimal multiplier 1.15.
terms.common_errors: Using 0.15 instead of 1.15 for an increase.

terms.definition: Breaking a number or expression into simpler parts to make calculation easier.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: Use prime factor decomposition to write 60 = 2² × 3 × 5.

terms.definition: To reach a conclusion by applying logical reasoning from given facts or rules.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: From the angle facts, deduce that angle x = 65°.

terms.definition: Based on logical reasoning from established rules and facts, leading to certain conclusions.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: Use deductive reasoning to prove this angle relationship.
terms.common_errors: Confusing deductive (from rules to conclusion) with inductive (from examples to pattern).

terms.definition: The unit of measurement for angles (°); also the highest power in a polynomial.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: This angle measures 45 degrees; this polynomial has degree 3.

terms.definition: To show or prove that something is true through mathematical working.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: Demonstrate that the sum of angles in a quadrilateral is 360°.

terms.definition: Having a lot of mass packed into a small volume; in maths, also refers to the rational numbers being dense on the number line.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: Iron is denser than wood — the same volume weighs more.
terms.common_errors: Confusing dense with heavy — a small dense object can be lighter than a large less dense one.

terms.definition: A compound measure: mass per unit volume, calculated as density = mass ÷ volume.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: If mass is 500g and volume is 100cm³, density = 5 g/cm³.
terms.common_errors: Confusing the formula — density = mass/volume, not volume/mass.

terms.definition: A decrease in value over time, often at a fixed percentage rate per year.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: A car depreciates by 20% per year: after 1 year, multiply by 0.8.
terms.common_errors: Confusing depreciation (decrease) with appreciation (increase).

terms.definition: A straight line connecting two non-adjacent vertices of a polygon.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: A rectangle has 2 diagonals; a hexagon has 9.
terms.common_errors: Counting sides as diagonals — diagonals only connect non-adjacent vertices.

terms.definition: A relationship where two quantities increase or decrease at the same rate: y = kx.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: If 3 apples cost £1.50, the cost is in direct proportion to the number of apples.
terms.common_errors: Confusing direct proportion (y = kx) with inverse proportion (y = k/x).

terms.definition: A number with a positive or negative sign, indicating its direction from zero.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: +5 and -3 are directed numbers.
terms.common_errors: Not distinguishing between the minus sign for subtraction and for negative direction.

terms.definition: Describing a relationship where one quantity is a constant multiple of another: as one doubles, the other doubles.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: Distance is directly proportional to time at constant speed.

terms.definition: A reduction in price, usually given as a percentage or amount off.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: A 20% discount on £80 saves £16, so you pay £64.
terms.common_errors: Adding the discount instead of subtracting.

terms.definition: Data that can only take specific distinct values, usually whole numbers; counted rather than measured.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: Number of siblings is discrete data — you can't have 2.5 siblings.
terms.common_errors: Confusing discrete (counted) with continuous (measured).

terms.definition: A visual way of presenting data, such as a table, chart, or graph.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: Choose an appropriate display for this data: a bar chart or scatter graph?

terms.definition: To show a statement is false by providing a counter-example.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: Disprove: 'all prime numbers are odd' — counter-example: 2 is prime and even.

terms.definition: To multiply each term inside a bracket by the term outside; the distributive property.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: Distribute: 3(x + 4) = 3x + 12.
terms.common_errors: Only distributing to the first term.

terms.definition: To share a quantity into parts that are in a given proportion.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: Divide £120 in the ratio 3:5 — each part is £15, so the shares are £45 and £75.
terms.common_errors: Not finding the total number of parts first.

terms.definition: Two parallel number lines with corresponding values, used to show proportional relationships.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: Use a double number line to compare litres and gallons.

terms.definition: A member or item in a set.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: 3 is an element of the set {1, 2, 3, 4, 5}.

terms.definition: To change the size of a shape by a scale factor while keeping the same proportions.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: Enlarge the shape by scale factor 3 from the origin.
terms.common_errors: Enlargement with a fractional scale factor actually makes the shape smaller.

terms.definition: A transformation that changes the size of a shape using a scale factor and centre of enlargement.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: Describe the enlargement: scale factor 2, centre (0, 0).
terms.common_errors: Thinking enlargement always makes shapes bigger — scale factor < 1 makes them smaller.

terms.definition: To input a value or expression into a calculator.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: Enter the calculation into your calculator.

terms.definition: An algebraic rule describing a straight line, usually in the form y = mx + c where m is the gradient and c is the y-intercept.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: Find the equation of the line with gradient 2 passing through (0, 3): y = 2x + 3.
terms.common_errors: Confusing gradient with y-intercept.

terms.definition: At the same distance from two or more points.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: Every point on the perpendicular bisector is equidistant from both endpoints.

terms.definition: The state of being equal in value, though possibly expressed in different forms.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: Show the equivalence of 3/4, 0.75, and 75%.

terms.definition: A ratio that represents the same proportional relationship as another, found by multiplying or dividing both parts by the same number.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: 3:5 and 6:10 are equivalent ratios.
terms.common_errors: Adding the same number instead of multiplying.

terms.definition: The range of possible true values for a number that has been rounded; expressed using inequalities.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: A length of 4.5 m (1 d.p.) has error interval 4.45 ≤ x < 4.55.
terms.common_errors: Using ≤ for the upper bound instead of <.

terms.definition: An approximation of the mean calculated from grouped data using midpoints of class intervals.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: Calculate the estimated mean by summing (midpoint × frequency) and dividing by total frequency.
terms.common_errors: Using class boundaries instead of midpoints.

terms.definition: For polyhedra: V - E + F = 2, where V is vertices, E is edges, F is faces.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: Check with a cube: 8 - 12 + 6 = 2. Euler's formula holds.

terms.definition: An answer left in its precise mathematical form (e.g. as a fraction, surd, or in terms of π) rather than a decimal approximation.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: Leave your answer as an exact value: the area is 25π cm².
terms.common_errors: Converting to a decimal when the question asks for exact.

terms.definition: A precise mathematical quantity without rounding or approximation.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: The exact value of sin(30°) is 1/2, not 0.5.

terms.definition: A set of events is exhaustive if at least one of them must occur; they cover all possibilities.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: Rolling 1-6 on a dice: these outcomes are exhaustive.
terms.common_errors: Confusing exhaustive with mutually exclusive.

terms.definition: To multiply out brackets in an algebraic expression.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: Expand 2(x + 3) = 2x + 6.
terms.common_errors: Only multiplying the first term in the bracket.

terms.definition: The theoretically predicted result based on probability calculations.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: The expected number of sixes in 60 rolls is 60 × 1/6 = 10.
terms.common_errors: Thinking the expected value must be a whole number.

terms.definition: The amount of money spent; total spending.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: Calculate the total expenditure for the school trip.
terms.common_errors: Confusing expenditure (spending) with income (earning).

terms.definition: A test or trial carried out to gather data, especially in probability.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: Perform an experiment: roll a dice 100 times and record the results.

terms.definition: The small raised number showing how many times a base is multiplied by itself; also called index or power.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: In 5³, the exponent is 3.
terms.common_errors: Multiplying the base by the exponent instead of raising to the power.

terms.definition: Growing or decaying by a constant multiplier; involving powers or indices.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: Exponential growth occurs in compound interest: amount = P × 1.05ⁿ.
terms.common_errors: Confusing exponential with linear growth.

terms.definition: To write a mathematical quantity in a specified form.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: Express 0.35 as a fraction: 7/20.

terms.definition: To convert a fraction or decimal into its equivalent percentage by multiplying by 100.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: Express 3/8 as a percentage: 3 ÷ 8 × 100 = 37.5%.
terms.common_errors: Dividing by 100 instead of multiplying.

terms.definition: To identify and pull out specific information from a table, graph, or dataset.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: Extract the median from the cumulative frequency graph.

terms.definition: To estimate a value beyond the range of known data by extending an observed trend.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: Extrapolating from the graph, the temperature at hour 10 would be about 35°C.
terms.common_errors: Treating extrapolated values as reliable — they are estimates.

terms.definition: An informal name for corresponding angles formed by a transversal crossing parallel lines, making an F-shape.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: F-angles (corresponding angles) are equal.
terms.common_errors: Not checking that the lines are actually parallel.

terms.definition: A diagram that breaks a number down into its prime factors by repeatedly dividing by prime numbers.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: Draw a factor tree for 60: 60 → 2 × 30 → 2 × 2 × 15 → 2 × 2 × 3 × 5.
terms.common_errors: Stopping before reaching all prime factors.

terms.definition: To write an expression as a product of its factors; the reverse of expanding.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: Factorise 6x + 9 = 3(2x + 3).
terms.common_errors: Not taking out the highest common factor.

terms.definition: Having equal probability for all outcomes; unbiased.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: A fair dice gives each number an equal chance of 1/6.

terms.definition: The specific outcomes that satisfy the condition of interest in a probability calculation.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: Rolling an even number: favourable outcomes are {2, 4, 6}.

terms.definition: A sequence where each term is the sum of the two preceding terms: 1, 1, 2, 3, 5, 8, 13, 21...
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: The next Fibonacci number after 8 and 13 is 21.
terms.common_errors: Forgetting that each term is the sum of the two before it, not just the one before.

terms.definition: Calculating a given percentage of a quantity, often using division by 100 and multiplication.
terms.subject: Mathematics

terms.definition: The initial value in a number sequence, often denoted a or u₁.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: In the sequence 4, 7, 10, 13, the first term is 4.

terms.definition: A mnemonic for expanding the product of two binomials: First, Outer, Inner, Last.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: Use FOIL to expand (x + 2)(x + 5): x² + 5x + 2x + 10 = x² + 7x + 10.
terms.common_errors: Missing one of the four products, especially the inner or outer terms.

terms.definition: A particular way of expressing a mathematical quantity, such as standard form, fraction form, or expanded form.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: Write 0.0034 in standard form: 3.4 × 10⁻³.

terms.definition: A structured logical proof using algebraic reasoning to show a statement is always true.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: Construct a formal argument to prove that (2n+1)² is always odd.
terms.common_errors: Using numerical examples instead of algebraic generality.

terms.definition: The plural of formula; mathematical rules expressed using variables and operations.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: These formulae will be given on the exam paper.
terms.common_errors: Using 'formulas' instead of 'formulae' in formal contexts (both are accepted).

terms.definition: To create a mathematical expression, equation, or formula to represent a situation.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: Formulate an equation from this word problem.

terms.definition: A calculator button (often S⇔D or a/b) for entering and converting fractions.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: Use the fraction key to enter 3/4 into your calculator.

terms.definition: Frequency divided by class width; used as the y-axis in histograms with unequal class widths.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: Frequency density = frequency ÷ class width.
terms.common_errors: Using frequency instead of frequency density on the y-axis of a histogram.

terms.definition: A table showing how often each value or range of values occurs in a data set.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: Record the results in a frequency table.

terms.definition: A rule that assigns exactly one output to each input value.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: The function f(x) = 2x + 1 maps 3 to 7.
terms.common_errors: Confusing functions (one output per input) with relations (can have multiple).

terms.definition: A diagram showing operations applied to an input to produce an output, used to visualise functions.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: Input 5 into the function machine: ×3 then +2 gives output 17.
terms.common_errors: Applying operations in the wrong order.

terms.definition: Every integer greater than 1 can be written as a unique product of prime factors (ignoring order).
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: By the fundamental theorem, 60 = 2² × 3 × 5 — this is the only way.

terms.definition: A formula for the nth term of a sequence that allows any term to be calculated directly from n.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: The general term of 5, 8, 11, 14 is 3n + 2.
terms.common_errors: Getting the wrong constant term.

terms.definition: To produce terms of a sequence or values of a function by applying a rule.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: Generate the first 5 terms of the sequence with nth term 2n - 1.

terms.definition: A sequence where each term is found by multiplying the previous term by a constant ratio.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: 3, 6, 12, 24 is a geometric sequence with common ratio 2.
terms.common_errors: Confusing with arithmetic sequences (constant difference).

terms.definition: Information that is provided or known in a problem.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: Given that x = 5, find the value of 3x + 2.

terms.definition: The steepness of a line; calculated as the change in y divided by the change in x (rise over run).
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: The gradient of y = 3x + 1 is 3.
terms.common_errors: Calculating run/rise instead of rise/run.

terms.definition: A unit of density measuring mass in grams per unit volume in cubic centimetres (g/cm³).
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: Gold has a density of about 19.3 grams per cubic centimetre.

terms.definition: A visual representation of the relationship between variables, plotted on axes.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: Plot the graph of y = 2x + 1.
terms.common_errors: Not labelling axes or using an appropriate scale.

terms.definition: The characteristic curve or line produced by a particular type of function when graphed.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: The graph shape of y = x² is a U-shaped parabola.

terms.definition: Using graphs or visual representations to solve problems or display information.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: Use a graphical method to solve these simultaneous equations.

terms.definition: Solving equations or inequalities by drawing graphs and finding intersection points or regions.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: Solve x² = 3x - 1 using a graphical method: plot both sides and find where they meet.
terms.common_errors: Not drawing graphs accurately enough to read solutions.

terms.definition: The relationship where a value is either larger than or the same as another; shown by the symbol ≥.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: x ≥ 5 means x can be 5 or any number greater than 5.
terms.common_errors: Confusing ≥ with >.

terms.definition: Data organised into class intervals rather than listed as individual values.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: With grouped data, we can only estimate the mean using midpoints.
terms.common_errors: Treating midpoints as exact values.

terms.definition: An increase in value over time, often by a fixed percentage; exponential growth.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: Population growth of 3% per year means multiplying by 1.03 each year.
terms.common_errors: Confusing growth rate with growth factor.

terms.definition: A logical connective meaning 'therefore' or 'as a result of this'; used in proofs and reasoning.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: The angles sum to 180°; hence the triangle is valid.

terms.definition: A polygon with 7 sides and 7 angles.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: A regular heptagon has all sides and angles equal.
terms.common_errors: Confusing with hexagon (6 sides).

terms.definition: A polygon with 6 sides and 6 angles.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: A regular hexagon has interior angles of 120°.
terms.common_errors: Confusing with pentagon (5 sides) or heptagon (7 sides).

terms.definition: The maximum or largest value in a data set.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: The highest value in the data is 98.

terms.definition: A graph for continuous data where bars have no gaps and the area of each bar represents the frequency.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: Draw a histogram with frequency density on the y-axis.
terms.common_errors: Confusing histograms with bar charts — histograms have no gaps and use frequency density.

terms.definition: A 10 × 10 grid showing numbers from 1 to 100, used for exploring patterns.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: Shade all the prime numbers on a hundred square.

terms.definition: The graph shape produced by inverse proportion (y = k/x) or reciprocal functions; has two separate curved branches.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: The graph of y = 1/x is a hyperbola.
terms.common_errors: Drawing the two branches as connected.

terms.definition: The longest side of a right-angled triangle, opposite the right angle.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: In Pythagoras' theorem, c is always the hypotenuse.
terms.common_errors: Using the wrong side as the hypotenuse.

terms.definition: A proposed explanation or prediction to be tested using data or mathematical reasoning.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: My hypothesis is that Year 8 scores are higher than Year 7.

terms.definition: Exactly the same in every way; congruent.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: These two triangles are identical — they are congruent.

terms.definition: An equation that is true for all values of the variable, such as 2(x + 3) ≡ 2x + 6.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: x + x ≡ 2x is an identity — it is always true.
terms.common_errors: Confusing an identity (always true) with an equation (true for specific values).

terms.definition: A logical structure for conditional statements: if a condition is met, then a conclusion follows.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: If a number is divisible by 6, then it is divisible by both 2 and 3.
terms.common_errors: Confusing the direction — if A then B does not mean if B then A.

terms.definition: A system of measurement using units like inches, feet, miles, pints, and pounds.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: Convert 5 miles to kilometres using the approximation 1 mile ≈ 1.6 km.
terms.common_errors: Mixing imperial and metric units in the same calculation.

terms.definition: Money received, especially as payment for work or from investments.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: Calculate the monthly income from a salary of £24,000 per year.
terms.common_errors: Confusing income (money in) with expenditure (money out).

terms.definition: A system of equations that has no solution because the equations contradict each other.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: y = 2x + 1 and y = 2x + 5 are inconsistent — parallel lines never meet.

terms.definition: In probability, events where the outcome of one does not affect the other; in variables, quantities that change separately.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: Rolling a dice and flipping a coin are independent events.
terms.common_errors: Treating dependent events as independent.

terms.definition: The power or exponent to which a number is raised; plural: indices.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: In 5³, the index is 3.
terms.common_errors: Using 'indexes' instead of 'indices' in maths.

terms.definition: Writing repeated multiplication using a base and an exponent, e.g. 2 × 2 × 2 = 2³.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: Write 5 × 5 × 5 × 5 in index notation: 5⁴.
terms.common_errors: Writing 5 × 4 instead of 5⁴.

terms.definition: The plural of index; the rules for manipulating powers (multiplication, division, raising a power to a power).
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: Apply the laws of indices: x³ × x⁴ = x⁷.
terms.common_errors: Adding indices when dividing instead of subtracting.

terms.definition: Cannot be divided exactly by a given number.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: 7 is indivisible by 2 because it leaves a remainder.

terms.definition: To draw a conclusion from data or evidence using reasoning.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: From the graph, we can infer that sales are increasing.

terms.definition: Without end or limit; continuing forever.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: There are infinitely many prime numbers.
terms.common_errors: Treating infinity as a number.

terms.definition: The value fed into a function or calculation; the x-value.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: When the input is 4, the output of f(x) = 2x + 1 is 9.

terms.definition: A whole number, which can be positive, negative, or zero (e.g. -3, 0, 7).
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: -5 is an integer; 3.5 is not.
terms.common_errors: Thinking integers are only positive.

terms.definition: To alternate between different topics or types of practice to strengthen learning.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: Interleave algebra and geometry questions in your revision.

terms.definition: The difference between the upper quartile (Q3) and lower quartile (Q1); measures the spread of the middle 50% of data.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: IQR = Q3 - Q1. If Q3 = 45 and Q1 = 20, the IQR is 25.
terms.common_errors: Confusing IQR with range.

terms.definition: The point where two lines or curves cross; in sets, the elements that belong to both sets.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: The intersection of lines y = x and y = 6 - x is the point (3, 3).
terms.common_errors: Confusing intersection (∩, 'and') with union (∪, 'or').

terms.definition: An operation that undoes the effect of another: addition/subtraction and multiplication/division are inverse pairs.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: To solve x + 5 = 12, use the inverse operation: subtract 5.

terms.definition: A relationship where one quantity increases as the other decreases at the same rate: xy = k.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: Speed and time for a fixed distance are in inverse proportion.
terms.common_errors: Confusing with direct proportion.

terms.definition: Describing a relationship where one quantity increases as the other decreases proportionally: y = k/x.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: Pressure is inversely proportional to volume at constant temperature.

terms.definition: Abbreviation for interquartile range: Q3 minus Q1.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: The IQR is a better measure of spread than the range because it ignores outliers.

terms.definition: A number that cannot be expressed as a fraction of integers; its decimal is infinite and non-repeating.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: π and √2 are irrational numbers.
terms.common_errors: Thinking 22/7 equals π — it's just an approximation.

terms.definition: A polygon where the sides or angles are not all equal.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: Most real-world shapes are irregular polygons.

terms.definition: A polygon where not all sides are equal and/or not all angles are equal.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: A rectangle is an irregular polygon because its sides are not all equal (though its angles are).

terms.definition: To rearrange an equation so that a specific variable is alone on one side.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: Isolate x: 3x + 5 = 20 → 3x = 15 → x = 5.
terms.common_errors: Applying operations to only one side.

terms.definition: A transformation that preserves distances — the shape and size remain unchanged (reflection, rotation, translation).
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: Reflections, rotations, and translations are all isometries.
terms.common_errors: Including enlargement as an isometry — it changes size.

terms.definition: A phrase used in mathematical reasoning to introduce a logical consequence.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: If x = 3, it follows that 2x = 6.

terms.definition: A logical connector in proofs meaning 'therefore' or 'as a consequence'.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: The sum is 180°; it follows that the third angle is 50°.

terms.definition: A compound unit of speed: the number of kilometres travelled in one hour (km/h).
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: The speed limit is 50 kilometres per hour.

terms.definition: A quadrilateral with two pairs of adjacent sides that are equal in length.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: A kite has one line of symmetry and one pair of equal angles.
terms.common_errors: Confusing a kite with a rhombus.

terms.definition: The agreed way of naming parts of shapes: vertices with capitals (A, B, C), sides with lowercase (a, b, c) opposite the matching vertex.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: In a triangle, side a is opposite vertex A.
terms.common_errors: Not using the convention consistently.

terms.definition: Rules for simplifying expressions involving powers: multiply = add indices, divide = subtract, power of power = multiply.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: Laws of indices: x³ × x⁵ = x⁸, x⁶ ÷ x² = x⁴, (x³)² = x⁶.
terms.common_errors: Adding indices when dividing instead of subtracting.

terms.definition: A relationship shown by ≤ where a value is smaller than or the same as another.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: x ≤ 10 means x can be 10 or any number less.
terms.common_errors: Using < when ≤ is needed.

terms.definition: A symbol used to represent an unknown or variable quantity in algebra.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: Let n represent the number of apples.
terms.common_errors: Treating letters as labels (a = apples) instead of variables.

terms.definition: Algebraic terms with the same variable raised to the same power, which can be combined by adding or subtracting.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: 3x and 5x are like terms; 3x and 3x² are not.
terms.common_errors: Combining terms with different powers (e.g. x + x²).

terms.definition: A restriction or weakness in a mathematical model, sample, or method.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: A limitation of this model is that it assumes constant growth.

terms.definition: A straight line drawn through the middle of data points on a scatter graph, showing the general trend.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: Draw a line of best fit and use it to estimate values.
terms.common_errors: Making the line pass through the first and last points instead of balancing through the middle.

terms.definition: A straight line with two definite endpoints; a finite portion of a line.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: The line segment AB connects point A to point B.
terms.common_errors: Confusing a line (infinite) with a line segment (finite).

terms.definition: Forming a straight line when graphed; involving variables to the power of 1 only.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: y = 3x + 2 is a linear equation.
terms.common_errors: Calling quadratic equations linear.

terms.definition: An equation where the highest power of the variable is 1, producing a straight-line graph.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: 2x + 3 = 11 is a linear equation.
terms.common_errors: Including x² terms — that makes it quadratic.

terms.definition: A function of the form f(x) = mx + c whose graph is a straight line.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: f(x) = 4x - 1 is a linear function with gradient 4.

terms.definition: The plural of locus; sets of points satisfying given conditions.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: Draw the loci of points equidistant from two given points.

terms.definition: The set of all points that satisfy a given condition or rule.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: The locus of points equidistant from A and B is the perpendicular bisector of AB.
terms.common_errors: Drawing a specific point instead of the full set of points.

terms.definition: The amount of money lost when an item is sold for less than its cost price.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: Bought for £50, sold for £35 — the loss is £15.
terms.common_errors: Confusing loss (selling below cost) with profit (selling above cost).

terms.definition: The smallest possible value of a rounded number; the start of its error interval.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: If x = 3.5 (1 d.p.), the lower bound is 3.45.
terms.common_errors: Confusing upper and lower bounds.

terms.definition: A small letter (a, b, c...) used in algebra for variables and sides of shapes.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: Use lowercase letters for sides: side a is opposite angle A.

terms.definition: The minimum or smallest value in a data set.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: The lowest value in the data is 12.

terms.definition: The size or absolute value of a quantity, without regard to direction.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: The magnitude of vector (-3, 4) is √(9+16) = 5.

terms.definition: Keeping an equation balanced by performing the same operation on both sides.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: To maintain equality, add 3 to both sides.
terms.common_errors: Changing only one side of the equation.

terms.definition: The number part (between 1 and 10) in standard form notation; the 'a' in a × 10ⁿ.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: In 3.4 × 10⁵, the mantissa is 3.4.

terms.definition: The ratio between distances on a map and actual distances in real life.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: A map scale of 1:50,000 means 1 cm on the map represents 50,000 cm (500 m) in real life.
terms.common_errors: Getting the ratio direction wrong.

terms.definition: The greatest value; the highest point on a graph or the largest number in a data set.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: The maximum value of -x² + 4 is 4, at x = 0.

terms.definition: A calculator function that stores a value for later use.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: Store the answer in memory using the M+ button.

terms.definition: A systematic approach or procedure for solving a mathematical problem.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: Show your method clearly — not just the answer.

terms.definition: The SI unit of speed: the number of metres travelled in one second (m/s).
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: The speed of sound is approximately 340 metres per second.

terms.definition: The decimal-based measurement system using metres, grams, and litres.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: Convert between metric units: 1 km = 1000 m.
terms.common_errors: Confusing metric with imperial units.

terms.definition: The smallest value; the lowest point on a graph or the least number in a data set.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: The minimum value of x² + 1 is 1, at x = 0.

terms.definition: A mathematical representation of a real-world situation, using equations, graphs, or diagrams.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: Use a linear model to predict future sales.
terms.common_errors: Treating the model as an exact description of reality.

terms.definition: The process of creating mathematical models to represent and solve real-world problems.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: Mathematical modelling involves making assumptions and testing predictions.

terms.definition: The value that occurs most frequently; another way of describing the mode.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: The most common shoe size in the class is 6.

terms.definition: The symbol × (or sometimes ·) used to indicate multiplication.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: In algebra, we often omit the multiplication sign: 3 × x is written as 3x.
terms.common_errors: Confusing × with the letter x in algebra.

terms.definition: Relating to multiplication; involving scaling or proportional reasoning.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: Percentage problems require multiplicative reasoning, not additive.
terms.common_errors: Using additive thinking when multiplicative is needed.

terms.definition: The number that, when multiplied by a given number, gives 1; the reciprocal (1/n for number n).
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: The multiplicative inverse of 4 is 1/4 because 4 × 1/4 = 1.
terms.common_errors: Confusing with additive inverse (negation).

terms.definition: Thinking about mathematical relationships in terms of multiplication and division (scaling, proportion, percentage).
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: Finding 25% requires multiplicative reasoning: multiply by 0.25.
terms.common_errors: Using additive reasoning when multiplicative is needed.

terms.definition: A connection between quantities where one is a constant multiple of the other.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: In y = 3x, x and y have a multiplicative relationship.

terms.definition: The number by which a quantity is multiplied, especially for percentage change calculations.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: A 15% increase has a multiplier of 1.15.
terms.common_errors: Using 0.15 as the multiplier instead of 1.15 for an increase.

terms.definition: To expand brackets by multiplying each term inside by the term outside.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: Multiply out: 3(2x + 5) = 6x + 15.
terms.common_errors: Only multiplying the first term.

terms.definition: Events that cannot happen at the same time; P(A and B) = 0.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: Rolling a 3 and rolling a 5 on one dice are mutually exclusive.
terms.common_errors: Confusing mutually exclusive with independent.

terms.definition: A positive whole number used for counting: 1, 2, 3, 4, 5... (some definitions include 0).
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: 7 is a natural number; -3 and 0.5 are not.
terms.common_errors: Including negative numbers or fractions.

terms.definition: A relationship where one variable increases as the other decreases, shown by a downward trend on a scatter graph.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: There is a negative correlation between temperature and heating bills.
terms.common_errors: Confusing negative correlation with no correlation.

terms.definition: A gradient less than zero, indicating a line that slopes downward from left to right.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: The line y = -2x + 5 has a negative gradient of -2.

terms.definition: The term that comes after the current one in a sequence, found by applying the sequence rule.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: In the sequence 3, 7, 11, 15, the next term is 19.

terms.definition: No apparent relationship between two variables; scattered points on a scatter graph with no trend.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: There is no correlation between shoe size and maths score.
terms.common_errors: Forcing a line of best fit through uncorrelated data.

terms.definition: Not forming a straight line; involving powers higher than 1 or other curves.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: y = x² is a non-linear equation — its graph is a curve.

terms.definition: A decimal that continues forever without ending, either repeating (rational) or not (irrational).
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: 1/3 = 0.333... is non-terminating but repeating.
terms.common_errors: Thinking all non-terminating decimals are irrational.

terms.definition: A polygon with 9 sides and 9 angles.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: A regular nonagon has interior angles of 140°.

terms.definition: A relationship shown by the symbol ≠, meaning two values are different.
terms.subject: Mathematics

terms.definition: A formula giving the value of any term in a sequence based on its position number n.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: The nth term of 5, 8, 11, 14 is 3n + 2.
terms.common_errors: Confusing the position (n) with the term value.

terms.definition: Known calculation results stored in memory, especially times tables and number bonds.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: Use number facts to speed up your mental calculations.

terms.definition: Relating to or expressed as numbers rather than letters or words.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: Give a numerical answer, not an algebraic expression.

terms.definition: The specific number that a variable or expression equals.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: When x = 4, the numerical value of 3x + 1 is 13.

terms.definition: A polygon with 8 sides and 8 angles.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: A stop sign is a regular octagon.

terms.definition: A symbol showing which mathematical operation to perform: +, -, ×, ÷.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: Choose the correct operator to make 8 __ 3 = 24.

terms.definition: Another term for inverse operation — the operation that undoes another.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: Subtraction is the opposite operation to addition.

terms.definition: The power of 10 closest to a number; used for rough comparisons of size.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: 5,000 has an order of magnitude of 10³.

terms.definition: A number written in its usual decimal form, as opposed to standard form.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: Write 3.4 × 10⁵ as an ordinary number: 340,000.

terms.definition: The starting amount before a change such as a percentage increase or decrease.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: After a 20% increase, £120 is the new price — find the original value.
terms.common_errors: Taking a percentage of the new value instead of working backwards.

terms.definition: The result produced by a function or calculation when given an input.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: For f(x) = 2x + 1, when input is 5, output is 11.

terms.definition: To place one diagram on top of another for comparison.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: Overlay the two frequency polygons to compare the distributions.

terms.definition: Notation for the probability that both events A and B occur.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: For independent events, P(A and B) = P(A) × P(B).
terms.common_errors: Adding instead of multiplying for 'and'.

terms.definition: Notation for the probability that event A or event B (or both) occurs.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: P(A or B) = P(A) + P(B) - P(A and B).
terms.common_errors: Forgetting to subtract the overlap.

terms.definition: Notation for the probability that event A does not occur; equals 1 - P(A).
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: If P(rain) = 0.3, then P(not rain) = 0.7.

terms.definition: The U-shaped curve that is the graph of a quadratic function y = ax² + bx + c.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: The graph of y = x² is a parabola opening upwards.
terms.common_errors: Not recognising that negative a gives an inverted parabola.

terms.definition: Lines in the same plane that never meet, always staying the same distance apart.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: Parallel lines have the same gradient.
terms.common_errors: Assuming lines that look parallel actually are without checking gradients.

terms.definition: A ratio comparing one part to another part (e.g. boys to girls = 3:2).
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: The part:part ratio of red to blue is 3:5.
terms.common_errors: Confusing with part:whole ratio.

terms.definition: A ratio comparing one part to the total (e.g. boys to total = 3:5).
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: If the part:part ratio is 3:2, the part:whole ratio is 3:5.
terms.common_errors: Confusing with part:part ratio.

terms.definition: The literal meaning of per cent: a number out of every 100.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: 25% means 25 parts per hundred.

terms.definition: A polygon with 5 sides and 5 angles.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: A regular pentagon has interior angles of 108°.
terms.common_errors: Confusing with hexagon (6 sides).

terms.definition: For each; used in compound units like km per hour or cost per item.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: The price is £2.50 per kilogram.

terms.definition: The symbol % meaning 'out of 100'.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: 75% means 75 out of 100.

terms.definition: The amount of change expressed as a percentage of the original value: (change ÷ original) × 100.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: From £80 to £100 is a 25% increase.
terms.common_errors: Dividing by the new value instead of the original.

terms.definition: Using percentages to compare proportions from different-sized groups on a common scale.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: School A has 60/200 = 30% pass rate; School B has 45/150 = 30% — the same percentage.
terms.common_errors: Comparing raw numbers instead of percentages.

terms.definition: A reduction in value expressed as a percentage of the original; calculated using multiplier (1 - rate).
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: A 20% decrease on £150: multiply by 0.8 to get £120.
terms.common_errors: Adding the percentage instead of subtracting.

terms.definition: An addition to a value expressed as a percentage of the original; calculated using multiplier (1 + rate).
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: A 15% increase on £200: multiply by 1.15 to get £230.
terms.common_errors: Finding the increase amount but not adding it to the original.

terms.definition: A part of a quantity expressed as a proportion out of 100.
terms.subject: Mathematics

terms.definition: A line that crosses another line segment at right angles (90°) through its exact midpoint.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: Construct the perpendicular bisector of AB using compass arcs.
terms.common_errors: Not passing through the midpoint, or not being at 90°.

terms.definition: To continue working through a problem despite difficulty; mathematical resilience.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: Persist with the problem — try a different approach if your first method doesn't work.

terms.definition: A function defined by different rules for different parts of its domain.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: A piecewise function might be y = x for x < 0 and y = x² for x ≥ 0.

terms.definition: An exact location in space, shown as a dot and often described by coordinates.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: Plot the point (3, 4) on the grid.

terms.definition: The exact point where two lines or curves cross each other.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: Solve simultaneously to find the point of intersection.
terms.common_errors: Giving only x or only y instead of the full coordinate.

terms.definition: A formula that calculates the value of a term directly from its position number in a sequence.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: The position-to-term rule for 4, 7, 10, 13 is 3n + 1.
terms.common_errors: Confusing with term-to-term rule (which uses the previous term).

terms.definition: Greater than zero; on the right side of zero on a number line.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: All square numbers are positive.
terms.common_errors: Including zero as positive — it is neither positive nor negative.

terms.definition: A relationship where both variables increase together, shown by an upward trend on a scatter graph.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: There is a positive correlation between height and hand span.
terms.common_errors: Confusing positive correlation with strong correlation.

terms.definition: A gradient greater than zero, indicating a line that slopes upward from left to right.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: The line y = 2x + 1 has a positive gradient of 2.

terms.definition: A calculator button (usually ^ or xʸ) for raising a number to a power.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: Use the power key to calculate 2⁸.

terms.definition: An index law: (aᵐ)ⁿ = aᵐⁿ — multiply the indices when raising a power to a power.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: (x³)⁴ = x¹² because 3 × 4 = 12.
terms.common_errors: Adding the indices instead of multiplying.

terms.definition: Any number of the form 10ⁿ: 1, 10, 100, 1000, etc.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: 10⁴ = 10,000 is a power of ten.

terms.definition: Numbers expressed with indices/exponents, e.g. 2³, 5²; also the rules for manipulating these expressions.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: Use the laws of powers to simplify x³ × x⁵.

terms.definition: Repeated work on a mathematical skill to build fluency and confidence.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: Regular practice of times tables improves recall speed.

terms.definition: The level of detail in a measurement or answer, indicated by decimal places or significant figures.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: Give your answer to 3 significant figures for greater precision.
terms.common_errors: Confusing precision with accuracy.

terms.definition: An estimate of a likely outcome based on patterns, data, or mathematical reasoning.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: Based on the trend, my prediction is that the temperature will reach 25°C.

terms.definition: A letter or group of letters added to the beginning of a word to change its meaning, e.g. 'milli-' means 1/1000.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: The prefix 'kilo-' means 1000, so 1 kilometre = 1000 metres.

terms.definition: A factor of a number that is itself a prime number.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: The prime factors of 30 are 2, 3, and 5.
terms.common_errors: Including non-prime factors like 6 or 15.

terms.definition: Writing a number as a product of its prime factors, often using index notation.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: The prime factorisation of 360 is 2³ × 3² × 5.
terms.common_errors: Not completing the process — stopping before all factors are prime.

terms.definition: The initial amount of money invested or borrowed, before interest is applied.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: The principal is £5,000, and the interest rate is 3% per year.
terms.common_errors: Confusing principal (money) with principle (rule).

terms.definition: The order in which operations should be performed, as defined by BIDMAS/BODMAS.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: Multiplication has a higher priority than addition.

terms.definition: A number line from 0 (impossible) to 1 (certain) on which probabilities are marked.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: Mark the probability of rolling a 6 on the probability scale at 1/6.
terms.common_errors: Placing values outside the 0-1 range.

terms.definition: Using mathematical knowledge and reasoning to find solutions to unfamiliar or multi-step problems.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: Problem-solving often requires choosing which techniques to apply.

terms.definition: A number expressed as a multiplication of prime numbers.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: Write 84 as a product of primes: 2² × 3 × 7.

terms.definition: For counting outcomes: if there are m ways to do one thing and n ways to do another, there are m × n ways to do both.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: 3 starters and 4 mains: product rule gives 3 × 4 = 12 meal combinations.
terms.common_errors: Adding instead of multiplying.

terms.definition: The money gained when an item is sold for more than its cost price.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: Bought for £30, sold for £45 — the profit is £15.

terms.definition: A logical argument demonstrating that a mathematical statement is always true, not just for specific cases.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: Write a proof that the sum of two even numbers is always even.
terms.common_errors: Using examples as proof — a proof must work for all cases.

terms.definition: Two quantities maintaining a constant ratio as they change.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: If cost is proportional to weight, doubling the weight doubles the cost.
terms.common_errors: Confusing proportional with equal.

terms.definition: Thinking about and solving problems involving ratios, fractions, percentages, and multiplicative relationships.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: Use proportional reasoning: if 3 apples cost £1.20, how much do 7 cost?
terms.common_errors: Using additive thinking when multiplicative is needed.

terms.definition: In any right-angled triangle, the square of the hypotenuse equals the sum of the squares of the other two sides: a² + b² = c².
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: Use Pythagoras' theorem: if a=5 and b=12, c=√(25+144)=13.
terms.common_errors: Using the theorem on non-right-angled triangles.

terms.definition: A set of three whole numbers that satisfy a² + b² = c², such as 3, 4, 5 or 5, 12, 13.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: 3, 4, 5 is a Pythagorean triple because 9 + 16 = 25.

terms.definition: An abbreviation from Latin 'quod erat demonstrandum' meaning 'which was to be demonstrated'; placed at the end of a proof.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: ...therefore 2n + 2m = 2(n + m), which is even. QED.

terms.definition: An expression or equation where the highest power of the variable is 2.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: x² + 3x - 10 = 0 is a quadratic equation.
terms.common_errors: Confusing quadratic (power 2) with cubic (power 3).

terms.definition: An algebraic expression containing a squared variable as its highest power, in the form ax² + bx + c.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: Factorise the quadratic expression x² + 5x + 6.

terms.definition: One quarter of a circle, forming a 90° arc.
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: Find the area of a quarter circle with radius 6: (π × 36)/4 = 9π.
terms.common_errors: Using the full circle formula instead of dividing by 4.

terms.definition: A value that divides ordered data into four equal parts: Q1 (25%), Q2/median (50%), Q3 (75%).
terms.subject: Mathematics
terms.example_usage: The lower quartile (Q1) is the value below which 25% of data falls.
terms.common_errors: Not ordering the data before finding quartiles.

terms.definition: An index law: aᵐ ÷ aⁿ = aᵐ⁻ⁿ — subtract the indices when dividing powers with the same base.
terms.subject: Mathematics
